query: recent studies on xaliproden and its effects on amyotrophic lateral sclerosis

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Riluzole, Edaravone, and sodium phenylbutyrate are the only approved medications to treat ALS but provide limited survival benefits. [1] [3] Riluzole, 50mg bid, is recommended for all patients with ALS and is thought to reduce glutamate-induced excitotoxicity. Riluzole is the only medication that improves overall survival (74% vs. 58% in the placebo group at 12 months) and slows the progression of symptoms (ie, survival without tracheostomy (57% vs. 50% at 18 months). Riluzole is most effective in patients who have been symptomatic for less than 5 years, have a vital capacity (VC) >60% of the predicted value, and do not require a tracheostomy. [36] It is important to note that the elimination of riluzole will be affected by CYP1A2 inhibitors like caffeine and theophylline (decrease the rate of elimination of riluzole). Transaminase elevation occurs in approximately 50% of patients. Monitor transaminases monthly for the first three months and every three months after.

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Pseudobulbar affect is commonly seen in PLS, and recognition with screening and early treatment with medications can reduce social withdrawal and positively impact patients' quality of life. Treatment with dextromethorphan/quinidine is commonly used. In patients who cannot tolerate dextromethorphan/quinidine or do not benefit from treatment, amitriptyline or fluvoxamine can be trialed. However, neither has been studied in controlled trials for the pseudobulbar effect. [1] Screening for mood symptoms, especially depression, is essential, and early referral to a psychiatrist or therapist is recommended when appropriate. Riluzole, a Food and Drug Administration-approved drug for ALS that provides a modest survival benefit of a few months, has not shown survival benefits in patients with PLS and is currently not recommended in the treatment of PLS. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium phenylbutyrate is a histone deacetylase inhibitor that decreases the stress response in the endoplasmic reticulum. Combined with taurursodiol (which increases the threshold for cellular apoptosis), it decreases neuronal destruction. The result is a slower decline in function. Sodium phenylbutyrate-taurursodiol is administered intrathecally and may result in myelitis, radiculitis, aseptic meningitis, and elevated intracranial pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: AVP-923 (dextromethorphan-quinidine 20mg/10mg) has proven effective in patients with pseudobulbar affect. [45] Less rigorous evidence has supported the use of amitriptyline and fluvoxamine in smaller trials. Pain is treated based on its underlying cause. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory medications, paracetamol, intra-articular steroids, or lidocaine injections are recommended for nociceptive pain, whereas gabapentin, pregabalin, tricyclic antidepressants, and morphine are advised for neuropathic pain. Mexiletine, levetiracetam, and quinine sulfate help patients with cramps. Assistive devices such as special mattresses, pillows, and wheelchairs may help to prevent pain. Ultimately, many patients require nonopioid analgesics and anti-inflammatory medications, and when these fail, opioids become the mainstay of pain treatment. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Edaravone is a free radical scavenger that is thought to reduce oxidative stress and slow functional progression. Edaravone provides the most benefit in patients with early ALS (ie, less than two years of symptoms, living independently, VC >80%, and scores of 2 or more in all items of ALSFRS-R) with probable or definite ALS (revised El Escorial criteria). One study showed approximately 33% slower functional decline in patients at 24 weeks follow-up. [37] Edaravone treatment is expensive ($146,000 annually). Edaravone should be used with caution in patients with asthma. It contains sodium bisulfite, which may cause asthmatic reactions in up to 5% of patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Motor Neuron Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Disease-modifying Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: The standard dose of Riluzole in the management of ALS is 50 mg two times daily. [58] The most common adverse effects include nausea, asthenia, and derangement in liver enzymes. [57] Pancreatitis and fulminant hepatic failure have also been reported as rare adverse effects. [59] Regular blood testing every month for the first three months, followed by three monthly for nine months and yearly, is recommended. [60] An increase of over three times the upper limit of normal in hepatic enzymes can be managed by dose reduction, followed by withdrawal followed by the re-introduction of the drug [60] . Riluzole is contraindicated in hepatic and renal impairments and in those who are breastfeeding and lactating. Further trials have been advised to test the efficacy of different dosing regimens. [61]

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: There is no cure for ALS. [5] [7] The median survival is 3 to 5 years. Approximately 30% of patients survive 5 years and 10% ten years. Riluzole provides a survival benefit averaging 3 months, with edaravone showing a 33% reduction in the decline of the ALS Functional Rating Scale–Revised (ALSFRS-R) at 6 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Motor Neuron Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Future therapeutic options
retrieved_docs.content: The use of antisense oligonucleotides in SOD 1 related and C9orf related ALS are subjects of research in future clinical trials. [93]

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Frequent and painful muscle spasms are common. Mexiletine (a sodium channel-blocking antiarrhythmic drug) is effective at a 150 mg BID dose. [40] [41] Other options include levetiracetam, gabapentin, baclofen, and tizanidine. [42] When oral therapy is impractical, botulinum toxin injections into the spastic muscles can be helpful. As weakness and functional decline inevitably progress, patients use assistive devices (canes, orthoses, crutches, and eventually wheelchairs), removable headrests in those with neck weakness, specialized utensils and holders, and eventually a pressure-relieving mattress with frequent repositioning to prevent pressure ulcers. [43] Sialorrhea is very common and can be treated with atropine, hyoscyamine, amitriptyline, glycopyrrolate, botulinum toxin injections into salivary glands, and even low-dose radiation therapy in those with refractory symptoms. [35] [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Evaluate the etiology of ALS. Identify the typical findings on the examination of a patient with ALS. Determine the interventions available for patients with ALS. Apply effective strategies to improve care coordination among interprofessional team members to facilitate positive outcomes for patients with ALS. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Tofersen -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Screen potential ALS patients with SOD1 gene mutations and disease severity who may benefit from tofersen treatment. Apply best practices for pharmacovigilance to identify and manage any adverse events associated with tofersen use in patients to avoid treatment-related complications. Assess the response to tofersen treatment by regularly evaluating ALS symptoms, disease progression, and changes in quality of life following the therapy. Communicate effectively with patients and their caregivers about the potential benefits, risks, and expectations of tofersen therapy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: One classic presentation of ALS may not exist. Personalized therapy needs to be implemented. [3] Each patient must be evaluated for ALS mimics. Treatment at multidisciplinary ALS clinics improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This topic will review the etiology, diagnosis, and evaluation of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). In addition, it outlines the treatments and interventions available. This topic highlights the role of the interprofessional healthcare team in evaluating and treating patients with ALS.

retrieved_docs.title: Tofersen -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Tofersen, a novel antisense oligonucleotide (ASO) drug, received accelerated approval from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in April 2023 for the treatment of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). ALS is a progressive, fatal neurodegenerative disorder affecting motor neurons in the brain and spinal cord, leading to muscle weakness, paralysis, and eventual death. Tofersen is specifically indicated for ALS patients with a superoxide dismutase 1 (SOD1) gene mutation. The mutant SOD1 protein resulting from this gene mutation gains a toxic function and significantly increases SOD1 messenger RNA (mRNA) levels and protein in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF). This activity provides comprehensive information about Tofersen's indications, contraindications, mechanism of action, adverse event profile, monitoring, and relevant interactions relevant to patients and healthcare team members involved in treating patients with ALS and related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Risdiplam - This drug is an oral medication that works via modifying SMN2 splicing and thereby increasing functional SMN protein levels. There have been numerous phase II/III trials that have shown efficacy with regards to improvement in motor function for patients with type II and III. Clinical trials are still ongoing and are currently under review in the US, Europe, and the UK for licensing. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2911
retrieved_docs.content: 4‑Aminopyridine is a pharmacologic agent that may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). By blocking voltage‑gated potassium channels, 4‑aminopyridine can improve nerve conduction and enhance motor function in patients with multiple sclerosis, thereby serving as a therapeutic option to alleviate some of the disease’s neurological symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1364
retrieved_docs.content: Baclofen and dantrolene are both pharmacologic agents that may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis. These medications are considered for managing the muscle spasticity and related motor symptoms that commonly occur in patients with multiple sclerosis, offering therapeutic options to improve functional ability and quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2912
retrieved_docs.content: Baklofen and dantrolen are both classified as centrally acting muscle relaxants, a pharmacologic group that exerts its effects on the central nervous system to reduce muscle tone. In addition to their shared classification, each agent may be used in the management of multiple sclerosis, where their muscle‑relaxing properties can help alleviate spasticity and related motor symptoms associated with the disease. Recognizing that both baklofen and dantrolen belong to the same therapeutic class underscores their potential interchangeability or complementary use in treating the neuromuscular complications of multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 90
retrieved_docs.content: Bromocriptine mesylate, bromodiphenhydramine, chlormethiazol, kabergolin, pergolid, piribedil, pramipexol, and tolcapon are all agents that may be used to treat Parkinson’s disease. In clinical practice, Parkinson’s disease is frequently associated with اضطرابات اكتئابية (depressive disorders) and رعاش (tremor), highlighting the importance of addressing both motor and non‑motor symptoms when selecting therapeutic strategies. The inclusion of multiple pharmacologic options reflects the heterogeneous nature of Parkinson’s disease and the need for individualized treatment plans that can alleviate tremor, improve motor function, and potentially mitigate associated depressive symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2082
retrieved_docs.content: Antiparkinsonika and gabapentin are noted to have a relationship, indicating that these two drug categories may be clinically connected, such as through co‑administration, potential drug interactions, or complementary therapeutic use in managing neurological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 89
retrieved_docs.content: Chlormethiazol is listed as a potential therapeutic agent for Parkinson’s disease, alongside other medications such as pergolid, pimavanserin, piribedil, pramipexol and tolcapon, each of which may be used to treat this neurodegenerative disorder. Clinically, Parkinson’s disease is associated with اضطرابات اكتئابية (depressive disorders) and رعاش (tremor), reflecting its common psychiatric and motor manifestations. Recognizing these associations and the range of drugs that may alleviate Parkinson’s disease symptoms is important for comprehensive patient management and for addressing both the motor and non‑motor aspects of the condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1451
retrieved_docs.content: Anticholinergic agents constitute a pharmacologic class that blocks acetylcholine receptors, and within this class the specific drugs profenamine, benzatropine‑methanesulfonate, biperiden, biperiden lactate, and trihexyfenidyl are recognized as child compounds. All of these agents share the anticholinergic mechanism of action and are employed in clinical practice for conditions such as Parkinsonian symptoms and drug‑induced extrapyramidal side effects. Understanding that profenamine, benzatropine‑methanesulfonate, biperiden, biperiden lactate, and trihexyfenidyl belong to the broader anticholinergic category aids clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy and anticipating class‑related adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 627
retrieved_docs.content: Bromocriptine mesylate, bromodiphenhydramine, chlormethiazol, kabergolin, pergolid, pimavanserin, piribedil, pramipexol and tolcapon are all agents that may be used to treat Parkinson’s disease, indicating a range of pharmacologic options for managing the motor and non‑motor symptoms of this neurodegenerative disorder. Clinically, Parkinson’s disease is also associated with depressive disorders (اضطرابات اكتئابية) and tremor (رعاش), highlighting the importance of addressing both psychiatric and movement‑related manifestations in patient care. Recognizing these therapeutic possibilities and associated clinical features supports a comprehensive approach to the diagnosis, treatment, and ongoing management of individuals with Parkinson’s disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1990
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B12 may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, indicating a potential therapeutic role for this vitamin in managing the neurological disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1298
retrieved_docs.content: Dantrolen and vitamin B12 are both identified as potential therapeutic agents for multiple sclerosis, suggesting that each may be used to help manage this demyelinating neurological disorder. Their inclusion highlights the exploration of diverse treatment options in the care of patients with multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039
retrieved_docs.content: Dantrolen and baklofen are classified as centrally acting muscle relaxants, a group of agents that reduce skeletal muscle tone by acting on the central nervous system. These medications are used to manage conditions characterized by excessive muscle contraction, such as spasticity and certain movement disorders, and their shared mechanism of central action underlies their therapeutic role in alleviating muscle-related symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Human corticotropin, methylprednisolon acetate, and methylprednisolone aceponate are all identified as agents that may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, indicating that several corticosteroid‑related medications are considered potential therapeutic options for managing this demyelinating disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2758
retrieved_docs.content: Baclofen, a GABA‑B receptor agonist commonly employed as a muscle relaxant, may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis. In patients with multiple sclerosis, baclofen can help alleviate spasticity and muscle stiffness, which are frequent neurological manifestations of the disease. By reducing excessive neuronal firing in the spinal cord, baclofen contributes to improved motor function and comfort, making it a valuable therapeutic option for managing the motor symptoms associated with multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2145
retrieved_docs.content: Bromodiphenhydramine and pimavanserin are pharmacologic agents that may be employed in the treatment of Parkinson’s disease. Bromodiphenhydramine, an antihistamine with anticholinergic activity, has potential utility for alleviating motor symptoms associated with the neurodegenerative disorder. Pimavanserin, a selective serotonin‑5‑HT₂A inverse agonist, is also recognized for its possible therapeutic role in Parkinson’s disease, particularly for managing psychotic features and may provide broader symptomatic benefit. Recognizing both drugs as possible treatment options expands therapeutic considerations for clinicians managing patients with Parkinson’s disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1977
retrieved_docs.content: Bromocriptine mesylate, chlormethiazol, and kabergolin are pharmacologic agents that may be used to treat Parkinson’s disease. Parkinson’s disease is clinically associated with اضطرابات اكتئابية (depressive disorders) and رعاش (tremor), reflecting its impact on both mood and motor function. In addition, Parkinson’s disease frequently co‑occurs with حالة نفسية غير طبيعية (abnormal mental states), underscoring the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of both neurological and psychiatric aspects in affected patients.
